---
title: "Banning a Dependency (Global Exclusion)"
tagline: "Or what to Do When a Library Must Never Appear Anywhere"
type: scenario
tool: sbt
---

#### ! Scenario

*Or what to Do When a Library Must Never Appear Anywhere*
This scenario demonstrates how to keep an artifact out of the *entire* build — not just out of one dependency's subtree ([SB5](../scenario-5-exclusions/guide.qmd)), and not just certain versions of it ([SB14](../scenario-14-reject/guide.qmd)). The classic drivers are security (an EOL library with known CVEs) and licensing (a GPL artifact arriving transitively).

::: {.callout-note title="Tool Versions"}
This scenario was tested with **sbt 1.12**, which uses the **Coursier** resolver (the default since sbt 1.3). Under the older Ivy engine (sbt < 1.3, or `useCoursier := false`) resolution edge cases differ and settings like `conflictManager` behave differently. `dependencyTree` is built in since **sbt 1.4**; earlier versions need the sbt-dependency-graph plugin.
:::

#### ! Example

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] --> LibA[lib-a:1.0.0]
    LibA --> LibB[lib-b:1.0.0]
    LibB -.->|excluded everywhere| LibC[lib-c:1.0.0]
```

```{.scala include="build.sbt" snippet="s18-deps"}
```

#### ! Expect
Exclusions in sbt are per-dependency (`.exclude(...)`, as in scenario 5), so banning `lib-c` means repeating the exclusion on everything that pulls it in.

#### ! Actual
`excludeDependencies += ExclusionRule("com.demo", "lib-c")` is a *setting*, not a decoration on one dependency: it removes `lib-c` from **every dependency's subtree**, including dependencies added later. Like Gradle's `configurations.all { exclude ... }` — and unlike Maven, which has no silent global prune at all — the removal is completely silent: no warning, no eviction notice, nothing in `sbt evicted`.

#### ! Why
`excludeDependencies` is applied during resolution to the whole graph, so it is path-independent — however `lib-c` arrives, it is pruned. The trade-off is the same as Gradle's: silence. If a library needs `lib-c` at runtime, the build stays green and the first symptom is `NoClassDefFoundError` in production, caused by one line in `build.sbt`. Maven users get the opposite ergonomics: the enforcer's `bannedDependencies` *fails the build* and names the offending path, but cannot remove anything.

#### ! Fix
*   **Build-wide exclusion (shown above):** right when you are certain nothing needs the artifact, or you provide a replacement (see [SB15](../scenario-15-substitution/guide.qmd)).
*   **Scala-suffixed artifacts:** for Scala libraries use the cross-versioned form, e.g. `ExclusionRule("org.typelevel", "cats-core_2.13")` or `excludeDependencies += "org.typelevel" %% "cats-core"` so the suffix tracks your `scalaVersion`.
*   **Shared policy:** in multi-project builds, put the rule in `ThisBuild / excludeDependencies` or a shared settings object so every project inherits the ban.

#### ! Risk
* LOW Excluding an artifact you have verified nothing needs at runtime, or that you replace with a bridge/fork.
* MEDIUM Rules accumulated in `build.sbt` with no comments; nobody remembers which are still needed.
* HIGH Excluding a library that *is* used at runtime — green build, `NoClassDefFoundError` in production, and no build-time hint of the cause.

#### ! Check

**Fast check:** `sbt dependencyTree`

**What to look for:** `lib-c` is absent from every subtree — with no marker of the removal. Comment the rule out and re-run: `lib-c` reappears under `lib-b`.

**Conclusion:** The rule silently prunes the artifact build-wide.

#### ! Scales
At organisation scale, build-wide exclusions belong in a shared plugin or common settings, with a comment per rule explaining *why* (CVE number, licence, replacement). Because the mechanism is silent, the comment is the only trace of intent — and a CI assertion that the banned artifact is absent from `Runtime / fullClasspath` recreates the tripwire that Maven's enforcer provides out of the box.

#### ! Takeaway
`excludeDependencies` is sbt's global ban: one setting, every path, forever silent. Comment every rule, and pair policy bans with a failing check — silence is the feature *and* the hazard.

#### ! Related
*   [SB5](../scenario-5-exclusions/guide.qmd) - Excluding a transitive from a single declaration.
*   [SB14](../scenario-14-reject/guide.qmd) - Keeping specific *versions* out while allowing the artifact.
*   [SB15](../scenario-15-substitution/guide.qmd) - Replacing a banned library instead of just removing it.

#### ! Wild

The commonest build-wide exclusions in the Scala ecosystem are logging bridges: banning `commons-logging` (replaced by `jcl-over-slf4j`) and `log4j:log4j` 1.x (EOL since 2015, unfixed CVEs) so that whichever framework drags them in, exactly one logging implementation reaches the classpath. The build-wide form is preferred over per-dependency `.exclude()` precisely because logging libraries arrive through *many* transitive paths.
